Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7 Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI

SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8, where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on that type) you may alternatively SSH to login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system but present additional development packages tuned to the node hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module (hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

user_input:
Hi ,
I am writing to seek your assistance with logging into the Dirac machine. I encountered a similar issue before.
I have completed all the initial setup steps and obtained the QR code from the web portal token some time ago. However, it has been a while since I last logged into the machine. While I can still access the safe account, I am unable to log in to the web portal( ( Link<[https://id.hpc.cam.ac.uk/auth/realms/CSD3/protocol/openid-connect/auth?response_type=code&scope=openid&client_id=login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk&state=D0OAg-WHtN1N7fTXG-9dpjl1oOY&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flogin-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%2Foidc&nonce=equ0MBTPKf0h82CBRborxJ-OMzVTwl-UTQPZ2TuGe4s](https://id.hpc.cam.ac.uk/auth/realms/CSD3/protocol/openid-connect/auth?response_type=code&scope=openid&client_id=login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk&state=D0OAg-WHtN1N7fTXG-9dpjl1oOY&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flogin-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%2Foidc&nonce=equ0MBTPKf0h82CBRborxJ-OMzVTwl-UTQPZ2TuGe4s)> ).
Could you please help me resolve this issue?
Thank you for your support.
Best regards,
Karthika
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / Web Portal Login (Dirac)
Technical Action Required: No (credential workflow guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User unable to log into web portal despite SAFE access. Correct flow: use SAFE-provided password and web TOTP (`CSD3:<userid>`). If login flow appears inconsistent, refresh/reset SAFE password and retry.

ACTION
1) Confirm user ID and expected credential pair (SAFE password + web token label).
2) If not prompted correctly or repeated failure, request password reset through SAFE.
3) Confirm successful login after reset.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Karthika,

Please use the SAFE-provided password together with the web token labelled `CSD3:dc-bhuv1`.

If this still fails, please request a new password through SAFE and retry.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish web TOTP label from SSH token label.

## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (IdP of last resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

user_input:
Dear HPC Support team,
could I receive advice on using checkpoints when running a slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2?
I have found the following documentation useful:[https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html)
sbatch
Several options have been added to support checkpoint restart:
- --checkpoint: Specifies the interval between periodic checkpoint of a batch job. By default, the job will have no checkpoints created. Acceptable time formats include "minutes", "minutes:seconds", "hours:minutes:seconds", "days-hours", "days-hours:minutes" and "days-hours:minutes:seconds".
- --checkpoint-dir:Specify the directory when the checkpoint image files of a batch job will be stored. The default value is the current working directory. Checkpoint files will be of the form "<job_id>.ckpt" for jobs and "<job_id>.<step_id>.ckpt" for job steps.
Environment variables are available for all of these options:
SLURM_CHECKPOINT is equivalent to --checkpoint:
- SLURM_CHECKPOINT_DIR is equivalent to --checkpoint-dir
however, the --checkpoint command does not work from a script of from terminal. According to the slurm-rest-23.02 update log (which I believe is the current version of slurm on HPC):
"* The checkpoint plugin interface and all associated API calls have been removed."
Does this mean it is not possible to use checkpoints on HPC?
Thank you for your help,
Lisa
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Resilience / Checkpointing Policy
Technical Action Required: No (capability clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asked about Slurm native `--checkpoint` functionality. Platform does not provide native Slurm checkpoint plugin support; users must implement checkpointing at application/workflow level. `dmtcp` module exists but compatibility with AlphaFold is user-dependent.

ACTION
1) Clarify no native Slurm checkpoint plugin on this cluster.
2) Recommend user-level checkpoint/restart implementation.
3) Mention `dmtcp` as optional tool to evaluate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Lisa,

Native Slurm checkpoint/restart is not enabled on this cluster, so `--checkpoint` options are not available here.

Checkpointing must be implemented at application/workflow level. We do provide a `dmtcp` module, though compatibility with AlphaFold would need to be tested in your workflow.

Kind regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply Slurm-native checkpoint flags are supported when plugin is absent.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Checkpointing / Slurm Script
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to use checkpoints with a Slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2, but the `--checkpoint` command does not work [1]. The user has found documentation on checkpointing, but it is listed as deprecated [1]. The user is looking for advice on how to use checkpoints with their Slurm script.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check if the user's application supports checkpointing natively, as some applications like GROMACS do [2].
2. If the application does not support checkpointing, consider using a checkpointing mechanism like DMTCP, which has SLURM support [3].
3. If DMTCP is not working, consider submitting the job to the long queue, which allows for longer job execution times [5][7].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about your application and its requirements?
- Have you tried using DMTCP or any other checkpointing mechanism?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your query about using checkpoints with your Slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2. We recommend checking if your application supports checkpointing natively. If not, you can consider using DMTCP or submitting your job to the long queue.
Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Checkpointing may not be available for all applications or queues [3].
- The long queue has restrictions and requirements, including a maximum job execution time of 7 days [5][7].
- Users must have a valid reason for accessing the long queue and may need to purchase additional resources [5][7].

## Running AlphaFold2 using Singularity on CSD3[¶](#running-alphafold2-using-singularity-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Load the Singularity image which exposes a run\_alphafold script into the environment. The script sets some default paths to the dataset.:

```
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity
```

Create a slurm script with the following contents to predict the structure of the T1050 sequence (779 residues). The script assumes that an input subdirectory exists containing T1050.fasta file:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A MYGPUACCOUNT
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH -t 04:00:00

# load appropriate modules
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity

run_alphafold \
--pdb70_database_path=/data/pdb70/pdb70 \
--bfd_database_path /data/bfd/bfd_metaclust_clu_complete_id30_c90_final_seq.sorted_opt \
--output_dir  $PWD/output/T1050 \
--fasta_paths $PWD/input/T1050.fasta \
--max_template_date=2020-05-14 \
--db_preset=full_dbs \
--use_gpu_relax=True
```

```
SIMAGE=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/singularity/images/alphafold-2.3.2.sif
# point to location of AlphaFold data
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data

  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \
  -B $DATA:/data \
  -B .:/etc \
  --pwd /app/alphafold \
  --nv ${SIMAGE} \

This page describes how suitable applications requiring unusually long execution times can be run on CSD3.

## Definition of long jobs[¶](#definition-of-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

We define long jobs as jobs requiring wall times (i.e. real execution times) of up to **7 days**. Continuous execution times of these lengths are normally disallowed by both non-paying and paying qualities of service (QoS), in order to achieve reasonable overall throughput.

In general it is advisable for any application running for extended periods to have the ability to save its progress (i.e. to checkpoint) as insurance against unexpected failures that may result in wastage of significant resources. Applications for which it is possible to checkpoint are largely immune from per-job runtime limits as they can simply resume from the most recent checkpoint in the guise of a new job. Applications for which it is not feasible to checkpoint may find the scheduling features described below to be of use.

Access to the long job QoS is not given by default; if you have a need to submit such jobs please contact support describing your case.

## The QOSL QoS[¶](#the-qosl-qos "Permalink to this headline")

Paying users with suitable applications may be granted access to the QOSL quality of service, which permits jobs running for up to 7 days. Jobs associated with this special QoS are confined to `-long` variants of the usual partitions (`icelake-long`, `cclake-long` and `ampere-long`). reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Software Build / NVHPC Toolchain
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The build is failing because the user is loading a very old NVHPC toolchain (`nvhpc/21.1`). The linker errors (`crtbegin.o`, `-latomic`, `-lstdc++`) indicate that this compiler stack is not compatible with the current Rocky8/Ampere software environment. The correct fix is to switch to the current Ampere base stack and a newer NVHPC module, then retry the install.

ACTION
1) Purge the current modules.
2) Load the current Ampere software stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/ampere/base`
   `module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`
3) Re-activate the virtual environment and rerun the `pip install` command.
4) If the build still fails, request the full updated build log and `module list` output.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Tom,

The failure is caused by the old `nvhpc/21.1` module stack rather than by the `jaxdecomp` package itself.

Please try:
`module purge`
`module load rhel8/ampere/base`
`module load nvhpc/25.3/gcc/nv5pif7f`

Then reactivate your environment and rerun the install.

If it still fails, please send the updated build log and the output of `module list`.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer the current Rocky8/Ampere base stack for Ampere GPU software builds.
- Do not recommend continuing with the deprecated NVHPC 21.1 stack.

user_input:
Dear HPC support team,
My name is Daniel Temko. I am a Research Associate in Prof. Jamie Blundell's group. I am currently trying to install a Singularity container to run AlphaFold3 on the HPC by using the attached Slurm script which I received from a recent CSD3-AI/ML clinic I attended. I am running into a problem (described below) and would be grateful for your help with trying to fix this.
Issue:
The script seems to have run successfully to install the container to /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3. However, when I navigate to this directory and try to run the container by calling "./run_alphafold3" I get the following error message:
FATAL: permission denied
I was wondering whether you have a sense of why this might be happening and what I would need to do to get this working without the error.
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Containers / AlphaFold / Singularity
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The AlphaFold3 container appears to have been created, but the user gets `FATAL: permission denied` when running `./run_alphafold3`. From the visible query alone, the safest interpretation is that the generated launcher script, its execute permissions, or the way it invokes the container needs checking. The next step is to inspect the wrapper script, confirm it is executable, and verify the image/data paths being used.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to show the generated launcher script and its permissions, e.g.:
   - `ls -l run_alphafold3`
   - `head -n 40 run_alphafold3`
2) Confirm the container image exists in the install directory and is readable.
3) If the launcher is not executable, instruct the user to run `chmod +x run_alphafold3`.
4) Review whether the launcher is invoking Singularity correctly for the local installation.
5) Confirm the required AlphaFold data/model weights are available and mapped as documented.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Daniel,

The `permission denied` error suggests the generated launcher script or the way it is being invoked needs checking.

Please send us:
- the output of `ls -l run_alphafold3`
- the first part of the script (`head -n 40 run_alphafold3`)
- confirmation that the AlphaFold image file exists in the install directory

If the launcher is not executable, please also try:
`chmod +x run_alphafold3`

Once we can see the wrapper contents and permissions, we can confirm the correct Singularity invocation.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume the container image is broken if the wrapper script was generated incorrectly.
- For AlphaFold3, confirm that model weights access has been approved separately.

Dear HPC support,
I would like to use the interactive Rstudio (via [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk<https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)>), but I need a newer R version. The version that it loads is 4.3.1, whereas I would like 4.5.1. Is it possible to get the interactive Rstudio to load this R version?
Thank you!
Best,
Heather
–
Heather Machado
Group Leader
CRUK Career Development Fellow
Department of Pathology
Department of Genetics
University of Cambridge
Cambridge, UK

Hi,
I am trying to run multi-xpu fine-tuning on the DAWN intel XPUs. I almost succeeded but there is an error raised.
According to Google, I have to install `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` to fix it.
[https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9](https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9)
The problem is that I do not have the sudo authentication.
Could you please help me install this package?
Thank you 
Best wishes,
Jiangnan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support

<MESSAGE id=0010 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Good morning,
I’ve managed to get a large proportion of our code codes ported to GPU using JAX, an appropriate framework for our needs, which shows excellent performance at large scale.

We provide a set of pre-populated Conda environments based on the Intel
Distribution for Python:

```
module av intelpython-conda
conda info -e
```

This module provides environments for PyTorch and Tensorflow.

Please note that Intel code and documentation sometimes refers to ‘XPUs’, a more
generic term for accelerators, GPU or otherwise. For Dawn, ‘XPU’ and ‘GPU’ can
usually be considered interchangeable.

## PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch on Intel GPUs is supported by the [Intel Extension for PyTorch](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-pytorch/xpu/latest/).
On Dawn this version of PyTorch is accessible as a conda environment named pytorch-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
```

Adapting your code to run on the PVCs is straightforward and only takes a few
lines of code. For details, see the official documentation - but as a quick
example:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex

...

# Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')

model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
```

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain

Hello HPC support team,

Name: Liam Collins-Jones, Account: lc984, PI: Gemma Bale
I would like to install Toast++ software (http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/research/vis/toast/download_src.html ). I attempted doing this myself towards the start of 2025 but was unsuccessful. Would anyone on your end be able to install this on my account? Happy to provide more details etc.
Thanks! Liam.

Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

1. Create a folder named `privatemodules` in the home directory with:

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

'text': 'If you wish to use the
older version of zlib then you must load it and then compile boost yourself.

If you wish to see what versions of libraries are hardcoded into a particular
Spack module then you must use Spack commands available after loading the
`spack` module, e.g.:

```
[auser@login-e-1 ~]$ module avail boost

------------ /lustre/sw/spack/share/spack/modules/linux-centos7-x86_64 ------------
boost-1.63.0-intel-17.0.2-fl25xqn boost-1.64.0-gcc-6.2.0-pftxg46

[auser@login-e-1 ~]$ module load spack

[auser@login-e-1 ~]$ spack find -dl boost
==> 2 installed packages.
-- linux-centos7-x86_64 / gcc@6.2.0 -----------------------------
pftxg46    boost@1.64.0
545wezu        ^bzip2@1.0.6
kskvysh        ^mpich@2.14
4og3my2        ^zlib@1.2.10

-- linux-centos7-x86_64 / intel@17.0.2 --------------------------
fl25xqn    boost@1.63.0
nq2yt4x        ^bzip2@1.0.6
jbjvxs7        ^zlib@1.2.10
```

This shows their are two boost modules installed (one for the Intel compilers
and one for the GCC compilers), they both depend on bzip2 1.0.6 and zlib 1.2.10
and the GCC version also depends on MPICH 2.14. The paths for these

'source_ranks': {'docs': 4}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# AlphaFold[¶](#alphafold "Permalink to this headline")

From the Alphafold website <https://github.com/deepmind/alphafold>

> This package provides an implementation of the inference pipeline of AlphaFold v2.0.
> This is a completely new model that was entered in CASP14 and published in Nature.
> For simplicity, we refer to this model as AlphaFold throughout the rest of this document.
>
> We also provide an implementation of AlphaFold-Multimer. This represents a work in progress and AlphaFold-Multimer isn’t expected to be as stable as our monomer AlphaFold system. Read the guide for how to upgrade and update code.

## AlphaFold data on CSD3[¶](#alphafold-data-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The 2.8TB dataset is stored in:

```
/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data
```

Note that you may need to ls the directory in order for it to be mounted. There are example sequences stored in the input directory.

The dataset has been recently updated (November 2023) so scripts may not work without pointing to new versions of the files. In addition, to address this [issue](https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold/issues/810), newer versions of the `uniref` can be found here /datasets/public/AlphaFold/data/uniref30/UniRef30\_2023\_02\*.

## Running AlphaFold2 on CSD3[¶](#running-alphafold2-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

There are various ways to run AlphaFold2 on CSD3. We encourage the use of:

To get up and running quickly on CSD3 it is possible to run the Singularity container provided as a module:

```
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity
```

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue is related to activating a Conda environment in a SLURM submission script [1][2][3][4][5][6][7]. The user is trying to activate a Conda environment named "ml" but the job is still using the base Conda Python [1]. Similar issues have been reported and resolved in the past, where the solution involved modifying the submission script to correctly activate the Conda environment [2][3][4][5][6][7].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should modify their submission script to include the following lines:
1. `module load miniconda/3`
2. `source ~/.bashrc`
3. `conda activate ml`

The `source ~/.bashrc` line is necessary to ensure that the Conda environment is properly activated in the non-interactive shell environment of the SLURM job [2][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact submission script you are using?
- Have you tried modifying the script as suggested above?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Stefanos,
To activate your Conda environment in the SLURM submission script, please modify your script to include the following lines:
1. `module load miniconda/3`
2. `source ~/.bashrc`
3. `conda activate ml`
This should resolve the issue and allow your job to use the correct Conda environment.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- It is essential to ensure that the Conda environment is properly activated in the SLURM job to avoid conflicts with other environments or packages [1][2][3][4][5][6][7].
- The user should be aware of the specific requirements for their Conda environment and modify the submission script accordingly [1][2][3][4][5][6][7].

If this is the first time using Anaconda Python then it is important to
run the first time `conda init` command to correctly prepare your shell
environemnt for using the full suite of `conda` commands. This only needs
to be done once.

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ which conda
/usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/conda
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda init
[... list of modifications not made ...]
modified      /home/user/.bashrc

==> For changes to take effect, close and re-open your current shell. <==
```

### Using Anaconda Python in SLURM scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda environments](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

Below we show a short example of creating a local Python environment and installing the
*biopython* package.

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Platform Incident / Filesystem Performance
Technical Action Required: Yes (investigate possible service-side performance issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user’s slow Python setup and `import torch` freezes could reflect either a problem in the user environment or a broader shared-filesystem or service issue. Because the slowdown affects multiple environment-creation methods and both home and `hpc-work`, the first response should check for any active service-side problem before focusing only on the user’s setup.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is a broader service issue affecting Python environment creation or shared filesystems.
2) Ask the user to confirm the login node and working path they are using.
3) If no active incident is found, collect the exact commands and timings needed to separate user-environment issues from platform performance issues.
4) Ask the user to retry once those checks have been completed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stelios,

The symptoms you describe may indicate a broader platform performance issue rather than only a mistake in your Python environment setup.

We are checking this on our side. In the meantime, please confirm which login node and working path you are using so that we can investigate further if needed.

Best regards,
HPC Support

The user has also tried following the instructions for setting up PyTorch on CSD3, but has experienced slow performance [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate the user's Python environment setup and configuration to identify potential issues [1].
2. Check the user's job submission scripts and parameters to ensure they are correctly configured for the CSD3 environment [2].
3. Consider recommending alternative methods for setting up and managing Python environments on CSD3, such as using virtual environments or conda environments [4].
4. If necessary, escalate the issue to a senior support specialist for further investigation and assistance [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your Python environment setup and configuration, including any customizations or modifications you have made?
- Have you tried running your jobs on a different partition or node to see if the issue is specific to a particular environment?
- Are there any specific error messages or output that you are seeing when you encounter the slow performance or freezing issues?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your assistance with this issue. I have tried setting up my Python environment using the instructions provided, but I am still experiencing slow performance and freezing when importing PyTorch. I would appreciate any further guidance or recommendations you can provide.
Best regards,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When assisting users with setting up and managing Python environments on CSD3, it is essential to follow established policies and procedures to ensure consistency and reliability [1].

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

## Running a PyTorch program[¶](#running-a-pytorch-program "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch will be able to run on both CPU and GPU.

CPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-CPU
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node 1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 8
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# Let PyTorch use all available CPU cores for OpenMP, MKL and MKL-DNN backends
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py
```

GPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-GPU
#SBATCH --partition ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#! change to gpu:4 to use all 4 GPU cards on a GPU node.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py

## Running Tensorflow in containers[¶](#running-tensorflow-in-containers "Permalink to this headline")

CSD3 also supports running inside apptainer containers. A Tensorflow container can be downloaded from the Docker Hub and run with:

```
# The next command generates a > 3.8 GB file in the folder it is issued. Use with care.
apptainer pull docker://tensorflow/tensorflow:latest-gpu
apptainer exec --nv ./tensorflow_latest-gpu.sif python ./helloworld.py

user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

The Singularity image was built from Deepmind’s docker script and has been tested on the A100 nodes. The MSA construction and model inference are done on the same node type - it isn’t easy to decouple the two steps without using separate implementations (see below). Users can choose to run on CPU but the inference step takes considerably longer than on a GPU. Running on a GPU means that the CPU preprocessing (MSA) step can dominate the running time (depending on the particular sequence we wish to predict the structure of).

## Current Issues[¶](#current-issues "Permalink to this headline")

We are aware of the slow preprocessing time of hhblits on CSD3 and are working to improve this. For the small database it’s possible to pre-stage the data on the local ssd drive (with rsync), but this is not possible for the full database as it exceeds the capacity of the local ssd drive.

## Appendices[¶](#appendices "Permalink to this headline")

Building the singularity image on CSD3:

```
git clone alphafold
cd ./alphafold
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t alphafold .

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain

user_input:
Dear support team,
I am trying to log in to my RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla. I am following the instructions given in "Reseach Storage Documentation”. Unfortunately, I am denied access after entering my password and the TOTP verification code (see screenshot).
I can succesfully log in to [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/), so I assume I have the correct password. I have also succesfully set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 (see screenshot), and I am correctly entering the code when prompted to do so.
Maybe I need a different password that is specific for this service?
Kind regards
Christina
Prof. Dr. med. Christina Zielinski
Professor of Infection and Immunity
Department of Pathology
University of Cambridge
Tennis Court Road
Cambridge CB2 1QP
cez22@cam.ac.uk
s[Screenshot 2026-03-08 at 09.42.33.png]
[IMG_5398.PNG]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues accessing their RCS storage using FileZilla, despite successfully logging in to the HPC login portal and setting up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 [1]. The error message indicates an authentication failure after entering the TOTP verification code [1]. This suggests a potential issue with the TOTP configuration or the authentication process.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify that the user has correctly set up TOTP on their account by following the instructions on the CSD3 website [2].
2. Ensure that the user is using the correct username, password, and TOTP code when attempting to log in to the RCS storage [1].
3. If the issue persists, try resetting the MFA on the user's account and reconfigure TOTP [3].
4. Provide the user with instructions on how to access the RCS storage using the command line tool, such as PowerShell or Terminal app [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have correctly set up TOTP on your account?
- Are you using the correct username, password, and TOTP code when attempting to log in to the RCS storage?
- Have you tried resetting the MFA on your account and reconfiguring TOTP?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Christina,
To access your RCS storage, please ensure that you have correctly set up TOTP on your account by following the instructions on the CSD3 website. If you are still experiencing issues, try resetting the MFA on your account and reconfigure TOTP. You can also try accessing the storage using the command line tool, such as PowerShell or Terminal app.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is aware of the importance of keeping their TOTP code secure and not sharing it with anyone [2].
- Verify that the user has correctly set up TOTP on their account before providing further assistance [2].

user_input:
Good evening,
I have recently been trying to connect to the CSD3 to start working in the cluster for the first time. I have Two-Factor Authentication on my account already enabled (please see image attached).
When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
Many thanks for your assistance on this matter.
Martin
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 January 2026 14:44
To: Martin Santamarina Garcia <ms3242@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (ms3242)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support
